title: San Diego Comic-Con Art Show quietly changes rules, banning AI bilge | Film Stories

San Diego Comic-Con Art Show quietly changes rules, banning AI bilge
San Diego Comic-Con will no longer allow AI-generated trash to be hung next to genuine artwork following a quiet change to its regulations.
Update:
Perhaps proving that pester power works, San Diego Comic-Con has quietly updated its exhibition rules, and will no longer allow AI-generated imagery to be displayed at its art show.
The move comes just over a week after SDCC’s Art Show guidelines said exactly the opposite – the initial rules for this year’s event were that AI-derived stuff “may be placed in the show, but only as Not-For-Sale.”
As covered by
Cartoon Brew
, the SDCC Exhibiting Your Artwork rules have now been amended.
One key point reads:
(3) Material created by Artificial Intelligence (AI) either partially or wholly, is not allowed in the art show. If there are questions, the Art Show Coordinator will be the sole judge of acceptability.
There has been considerable online backlash against the previous set of guidelines, which appeared to be put in place two years ago, but subsequently came to light as negative sentiment towards AI-generated material has grown.
SDCC hasn’t stated anything publicly about this change, at least at the time of writing. We’ve contacted SDCC and will update this post if we get a response.
In the meantime, flesh-and-blood artists – who train and refine their craft for years, rather than type prompts into a computer – can take some solace that rectangles of AI-generated bilge won’t appear next to work at this year’s SDCC.
Our previous story follows…
14th January 2026:
In a year where generally comic cons and fan events have worked hard to draw a line where
generative AI
is concerned, the biggest of them all appears to have sold artists out.
Suggested product

San Diego Comic-Con has long been a showcase for artists and their work, from well before it became an event about major panels for movie and TV companies. It’s been a place where artists can push and promote their work. But at the 2026 event, said artists may now find themselves next to someone who’s used generative AI to create their work. Generative AI that feeds off oftentimes copyrighted work with no recompense to its creator. And that’s before you get to the environmental impact of its digital plagiarism too.
By San Diego Comic-Con’s own figures, ‘more than 800 iterms were sold by the 116 artists exhibiting in the Comic-Con Art Show’.
An update to its terms and conditions for the upcoming summer 2026 reads:
(3) Material produced by Artificial Intelligence (AI) may be placed in the show, but only as Not-for-Sale (NFS). It must be clearly marked as AI-produced, not simply listed as a print. If one of the parameters in its creation was something similar to “Done in the style of <specific artist>,” that information must be added to the description. If there are questions, the Art Show Coordinator will be the sole judge of acceptability.
It’s not a good paragraph to read, and seems – to my tired, early morning eyes – to mean that SDCC will allow not just AI slop, but AI slop tailored to mimic the style of an actual working artist. A clause that surely should have rendered a piece of work inadmissible is now down to the art show coordinator to see whether they can get through.
Laughably, the very next clause in the T&Cs is about copyright and crediting the owner and creator of, say, the creator of Superman if you use the Man Of Steel in your work. I can see no such clause in the AI paragraph.
Bizarre, folks, and it feels like San Diego Comic-Con has chosen to side with the evil empire here, rather than the Rebel Alliance. Hopefully, common sense might ultimately prevail, but I ain’t holding my breath.
